title: Why cold exposure works for some survivors and harms others
author: Kalesh
excerpt:

The water is 38 degrees. Your body knows what to do before your mind catches up. 

 I stood at the edge of a frozen lake in upstate New York last January,...
htmlContent:
The water is 38 degrees. Your body knows what to do before your mind catches up.

I stood at the edge of a frozen lake in upstate New York last January, wearing nothing but a swimsuit and a thin layer of desperation. My friend had been raving about cold exposure for months. "It changed my life," she said. "My anxiety dropped by 80 percent. I sleep like a baby. You have to try it."

So I tried it.

I lowered myself into that water and felt my breath seize. My heart hammered against my ribs. Every muscle in my body contracted at once. I lasted maybe 45 seconds before scrambling out, teeth chattering, skin burning, and I felt... nothing. Not euphoria. Not clarity. Just cold. And a vague sense of shame that I couldn't access the transformation everyone promised.

I tried again the next week. And the week after that. Each time, my nervous system responded like I was being attacked. Because for me, it was.

Here's the thing. Cold exposure works beautifully for some people. It reduces inflammation, sharpens focus, builds resilience. But for others, it triggers a cascade of physiological stress that can worsen trauma symptoms, dysregulate the nervous system, and even lead to long-term setbacks. The difference isn't willpower. It's not about "pushing through." It's about what's happening underneath your conscious awareness.

Let me explain why.

What cold exposure actually does to your nervous system

When you immerse yourself in cold water, your body activates what Stephen Porges calls the ventral vagal freeze response. Actually, that's not quite right. Porges, the neuroscientist who developed Polyvagal Theory, would tell you that cold water triggers a rapid shift from ventral vagal safety to a survival state. Your body doesn't know you chose to be in that water. It just knows it's cold, and cold means danger.

I want to talk about something that doesn't get enough attention. The way chronic stress changes your relationship with pain. When your nervous system has been in overdrive for years, your pain threshold shifts. Things that shouldn't hurt, hurt. Things that should hurt, don't register at all. Your body's volume knob is broken.

This is what the research on central sensitization shows. The spinal cord and brain start amplifying pain signals. Fibromyalgia, chronic fatigue, irritable bowel - these aren't imaginary. They're the body's honest response to a nervous system that never got to complete its stress cycle. The energy that was meant for fight or flight got trapped. And trapped energy has to go somewhere.

Sit with that for a moment. If you've been told your pain isn't real, if you've been dismissed by doctors, if you've started to doubt your own body - I want you to know something. Your body isn't lying. It's speaking the only language it has. The question isn't whether the pain is real. The question is what your body is trying to tell you through it.

Your heart rate spikes. Cortisol floods your system. Blood vessels constrict. Your brain releases norepinephrine and dopamine, which is why some people feel alert and euphoric afterward. But here's what the wellness industry doesn't tell you: for a traumatized nervous system, this cascade can look identical to a panic attack.

Think about it. If your baseline is already hypervigilant, already bracing for threat, adding more physiological stress doesn't build resilience. It pushes you past your window of tolerance. Dan Siegel coined that term, and it's crucial here. Your window of tolerance is the zone where you can process experience without becoming overwhelmed. Cold water can shrink that window if your system is already on edge.

I'm not saying cold exposure is bad. I'm saying it's not neutral. It's a powerful intervention that works differently depending on your nervous system's history. For someone with a secure attachment history and a well-regulated ventral vagal state, cold water is a challenge they can meet. For someone with complex trauma, it can be a trigger that sends them into dorsal vagal shutdown.

Notice what just happened in your body. Did reading that sentence make you feel tight? Did your shoulders rise toward your ears? That's your system telling you something.

Why some survivors get worse instead of better

I worked with a client last year who had been doing daily cold plunges for six months. She was proud of her discipline. But she was also exhausted, irritable, and having more flashbacks than ever. She thought she was failing at cold exposure. She was actually succeeding at retraumatizing herself.

Here's the mechanism. When you have a history of trauma, your nervous system has learned that survival requires constant vigilance. Your threat detection is dialed up to 11. When you add cold water, you're not teaching your system safety. You're confirming that the world is a dangerous place where your body is under attack. Your system doesn't distinguish between a voluntary cold plunge and an actual threat. It just knows it's cold, and cold has historically meant death.

Bessel van der Kolk, author of "The Body Keeps the Score," spent decades documenting how trauma lives in the body. He showed that traumatized people often lose the ability to accurately sense what's happening inside themselves. So when cold water triggers a stress response, they might not recognize it as stress. They might interpret it as "this is working" or "I'm building grit." Meanwhile, their cortisol is spiking, their inflammation is increasing, and their nervous system is becoming more dysregulated.

I want to pause here and check in with you. How's your body doing right now? Not your mind - your body. Is there tension somewhere? A heaviness? A restlessness? Whatever you find, it's information. It's your nervous system responding to what you're reading. And that response is valid.

One thing I've learned in my own recovery is that reading about trauma can be activating. Your body doesn't always know the difference between remembering something and experiencing it. So if you need to take a break, take a break. Put your feet on the floor. Look around the room. Name five things you can see. This isn't avoidance. This is regulation. There's a difference.

A book that connects the dots between stress and illness is When the Body Says No (paid link). Gabor Mate, the physician who wrote it, argues that chronic stress and suppressed emotions directly contribute to disease. Cold exposure, when mismatched to your nervous system, becomes just another form of suppression. You override your body's signals in the name of wellness. You tell yourself to push through. And your body keeps score.

I see this pattern constantly. People who grew up in chaotic or abusive environments learned to disconnect from their bodies. It was a survival strategy. Cold exposure can feel like a continuation of that disconnection. You're training yourself to ignore pain, to override discomfort, to keep going when every signal says stop. That's not healing. That's more of the same.

Stay with me here. I'm not saying you're broken or that cold exposure is harmful. I'm saying we need to stop pretending there's a one-size-fits-all protocol for nervous system health.

The missing piece: what your body actually needs

If cold exposure isn't right for you, what do you do instead? The answer depends on your nervous system's current state. But here's a starting point: warmth, safety, and predictability.

For a traumatized system, the first goal isn't resilience. It's regulation. You need to teach your body that it's safe enough to rest, to digest, to feel warmth without bracing for impact. This is where Peter Levine's work on somatic experiencing becomes invaluable. He showed that trauma gets stuck in the body as incomplete survival responses. The way out isn't more stress. It's completing those responses in a safe, contained way.

One supplement that many people find calming for their nervous system is Doctors Best Magnesium Glycinate (paid link). Magnesium supports the GABA receptors in your brain, which help your nervous system settle. It's not a cure, but it can create a foundation of calm that makes other interventions possible.

Another tool that works for many survivors is gentle, predictable pressure. A weighted blanket that many find calming for their nervous system is YnM Weighted Blanket 15 lbs (paid link). The weight stimulates your proprioceptive system, which can activate the ventral vagal pathway and signal safety. It's the opposite of cold water. It's containment, not challenge.

Something happens in the body when safety has been absent for a long time. The muscles forget how to relax. Not metaphorically - literally. The fascia tightens. The diaphragm restricts. The psoas - that deep hip flexor that connects your spine to your legs - stays chronically contracted, ready to run or curl into a ball at any moment.

Bessel van der Kolk's research on yoga and trauma showed something worth noticing. Traumatized people who practiced yoga showed changes in their brain scans - specifically in the insula, the part of the brain responsible for interoception, for feeling what's happening inside your own body. Many trauma survivors have learned to disconnect from body sensation entirely. It was too dangerous to feel. Yoga - gentle, trauma-informed yoga - helped them come back.

I'm not saying yoga fixes everything. I'm saying the body needs to be included in healing. You can't think your way out of something that lives below thought. You have to feel your way through it. Slowly. With support. At your own pace. There's no rushing this.

A practical guide to working with your nervous system is Anchored (paid link) by Deb Dana. She's a clinician who applies Polyvagal Theory in practical ways. The book walks you through mapping your nervous system, finding your glimmers, and building capacity without pushing past your limits. No cold water required.

I'm not saying you can never do cold exposure. I'm saying you might need to build capacity first. Think of it like weightlifting. You don't start with your max. You start with what you can handle, and you slowly increase. For a traumatized nervous system, cold water might be a max lift, not a warm-up.

How to know if cold exposure is right for you

Here's a simple test. Before you get in cold water, check in with your body. What's your baseline? Are you already feeling anxious, tired, or on edge? If so, cold exposure will likely that. If you're feeling grounded, safe, and resourced, it might be a useful challenge.

After you get out, pay attention to how you feel for the next few hours. Do you feel energized and clear? Or do you feel shaky, irritable, or numb? Your nervous system is giving you data. Trust it more than the influencers.

Wild, right? The wellness industry sells us protocols that work for some and harm others, and we're left feeling like failures when our bodies don't cooperate. But your body isn't failing. It's protecting you. It's doing exactly what it learned to do to keep you alive.

If you're a survivor and cold exposure hasn't worked for you, I want you to hear this: you're not weak. You're not doing it wrong. Your nervous system is responding to a perceived threat in the only way it knows how. The healing work isn't about overriding that response. It's about creating enough safety that your system can eventually let its guard down.

That might mean warm baths instead of cold plunges. Gentle movement instead of intense exercise. Rest instead of pushing through. And that's okay. That's not failure. That's wisdom.

Sit with that. Let it land. Your healing doesn't need to look like anyone else's.
